title: Ancient Snake Fossil Rewrites Evolution: It Had Legs and a Bone Scientists Got Wrong for 160 Years
summary:
Imagine a snake with tiny hind legs and a cheekbone — sounds like something from a fantasy movie, right? But that's exactly what a 100-million-year-old fossil from Argentina has revealed. Meet Najash rionegrina, an ancient rear-limbed snake that lived during the Cretaceous period, when dinosaurs still roamed the Earth.

Scientists used a technique called micro-computed tomography (micro-CT scanning) to look inside the fossil without damaging it. This allowed them to reconstruct the skull in incredible detail, including the paths of nerves and blood vessels. What they found was shocking: this ancient snake still had a jugal bone, or cheekbone, a feature that has almost completely disappeared in living snakes.

"After 160 years of getting it wrong, this paper corrects this very important feature based not on guesswork, but on empirical evidence," said Michael Caldwell, a professor at the University of Alberta and co-author of the study.

The discovery challenges a long-held theory about snake origins. Instead of evolving from small burrowing animals, the evidence from Najash suggests that the ancestors of modern snakes were larger-bodied creatures with wide mouths. "Our findings support the idea that the ancestors of modern snakes were big-bodied and big-mouthed — instead of small burrowing forms as previously thought," explained Fernando Garberoglio, lead author of the study.

The fossil also shows that early snakes held onto their hindlimbs for an extended period before eventually losing them. This "snake in transition" provides a crucial snapshot of evolution in action.

Later studies have added more twists to the story. In 2020, a fossil of a blind snake from Brazil showed that some early blind snakes were over 1 meter long — much bigger than their living relatives. And a 2025 study found a Jurassic squamate from Scotland with a mix of lizard-like and snake-like traits, suggesting that early snake evolution was complex and involved a lot of experimentation.

Even with these new discoveries, Najash remains one of the clearest windows into a crucial stage of snake evolution. It captures a moment when snakes still had hindlimbs, still retained a more lizard-like skull, and had not yet fully acquired the body plan seen in their modern descendants. That combination is exactly what makes this fossil so valuable — it doesn't just show an ancient snake, it shows an ancient snake in transition.
why_it_matters: This discovery is important because it forces scientists to rethink long-held theories about snake evolution, showing that scientific understanding can change with new evidence. For students, it demonstrates how fossils actively reshape our knowledge of life's history.

keywords.explanation: The ancient rear-limbed snake fossil from Argentina.
The preserved remains of an ancient organism.
Back legs that early snakes retained for a long time.
The cheekbone that was thought to be lost in snakes but found in Najash.
A technique that creates detailed 3D images of fossils without damaging them.
The time period (145-66 million years ago) when Najash lived.

questions.question: What is the name of the ancient snake?
What technique allowed scientists to see inside the fossil?
Which bone was thought to be missing in snakes for 160 years but was found in Najash?
What time period did Najash live during?
What did the fossil suggest about snake ancestors' body size?
Who is one of the co-authors of the study?

background_read: The Cretaceous period (145-66 million years ago) was the time when dinosaurs thrived and flowering plants appeared.
Snakes are thought to have evolved from lizards, but the exact path is debated.
Earlier theories suggested snakes evolved from small burrowing lizards, but Najash challenges that.
The jugal bone is part of the cheek region and is absent in most modern snakes; its presence in Najash shows a more lizard-like skull.
Micro-CT scanning creates detailed 3D images without damaging the fossil, allowing scientists to see internal structures.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Imagines a snake with legs and cheekbone
└─ specific: 'Meet Najash rionegrina, an ancient rear-limbed snake'
METHOD: Micro-CT scanning to reconstruct skull
└─ detail: 'paths of nerves and blood vessels'
KEY FINDING: Presence of jugal bone (cheekbone) corrects 160-year error
├─ Quote: 'After 160 years of getting it wrong... empirical evidence'
└─ Challenge to theory: big-bodied and big-mouthed ancestors vs small burrowing
EVIDENCE: Hindlimbs retained for long period
LATER CONTEXT: 2020 blind snake fossil (Brazil), 2025 Jurassic squamate (Scotland)
SIGNIFICANCE: Najash remains clearest window into crucial transition stage

perspectives.description: They are thrilled because the fossil provides concrete evidence that contradicts decades-old assumptions. The detailed CT imaging allows them to see features never before detected, opening new avenues for research.

They see this as a key piece in the puzzle of snake origins, but caution that one fossil does not rewrite the entire story. More specimens are needed to confirm whether the big-bodied ancestor hypothesis applies to all snakes.
This discovery offers a compelling case study to teach how science progresses through empirical evidence. It can inspire students to question established ideas and value new data.

Some may misinterpret the fossil as evidence that evolution is uncertain or that scientists 'don't know what they're talking about.' However, the correction actually shows how science self-corrects with better evidence.
